verses.commentary: Descent from the mountain — words give way to deeds. Sermon completed; Matthew 8-9 collects ten miracles in three groups, demonstrating the authority just claimed (7:29).
Leper (lepros, λεπρός) — ritually unclean, socially exiled (Leviticus 13). Kneels and addresses Jesus as Lord. 'If you are willing' — submission to Jesus' will is the petition's frame.
Jesus touches him — Torah said the unclean contaminates the clean. Here cleanliness contaminates uncleanness in reverse. 'I am willing. Be clean' — two words and disease yields.

Tell no one, but show the priest (Leviticus 14). Legal validation matters; the priestly certification re-integrates the man socially. The miracle is also a 'testimony' (martyrion, μαρτύριον) to the priests of who Jesus is.
Centurion — Roman officer, Gentile, commander of 100. The first Gentile to approach Jesus directly in Matthew. He comes pleading, not commanding.
His servant (pais, παῖς — child or young servant) paralyzed, in agony. The centurion crosses ethnic and class lines for a sufferer in his household.
Jesus offers to come — or possibly questions ('Shall I come and heal him?'). Either way, the centurion's response will be the turning point.
'Not worthy to have you under my roof' — acute awareness of Jew-Gentile distance. 'Just say the word' — faith in command alone, no physical contact needed.
Military reasoning: he himself is under authority and exercises authority. Therefore Jesus' word over disease should function like his own command over soldiers. Faith reasons from analogy.

Jesus 'marveled' (ethaumasen, ἐθαύμασεν) — one of two places in the Gospels he marvels (the other: unbelief in Nazareth, Mark 6:6). Such faith not found in Israel — a Gentile outflanks the covenant people.
East and west — Gentile inclusion announced. Many will recline at table with Abraham, Isaac, Jacob. The messianic banquet expands beyond ethnic Israel (Isaiah 25:6).
'Sons of the kingdom' (those who assumed birthright entry) thrown into outer darkness. Weeping and gnashing of teeth — Matthew's signature image of exclusion (8:12; 13:42; 22:13; 25:30).
'As you have believed' — faith calibrates outcome. The servant healed at that hour. Distance does not weaken the word.
Peter's house in Capernaum, his mother-in-law sick with fever. Peter is married (compare 1 Corinthians 9:5). Domestic detail, intimate scene.
Jesus touches her hand — physical contact, immediate cure. She rises and serves (diekonei, διηκόνει) him. Healing leads to service — the kingdom pattern.
Evening (Sabbath ended) — many brought. He drives out spirits 'with a word' (logo, λόγῳ) and heals all the sick. Authority verbalized; mass scale, no exhaustion of power.
Isaiah 53:4 quoted — 'he took up our infirmities and bore our diseases.' The Servant's substitutionary work begins in his healings, not only at the cross. Atonement reaches downstream into bodies.
Crowd pressure prompts withdrawal across the lake. Jesus moves toward Gentile territory and away from spectacle. The crowd is a hazard, not just an opportunity.
A scribe (grammateus, γραμματεύς) volunteers as disciple. Calls Jesus 'Teacher' — title respectful but inadequate. Says he will follow anywhere.
First Son of Man saying in Matthew. Foxes have dens, birds have nests — even animals have homes. The Son of Man has no place to lay his head. Discipleship means homelessness with him.
'Let me first bury my father.' Likely not an imminent funeral but waiting until father dies — possibly years away. The cultural duty is real and weighty.
'Let the dead bury their own dead' — the spiritually dead can handle the physical burials. The kingdom's claim overrides even foundational filial obligation. Discipleship is now.
Into the boat with disciples. The scene shifts to sea — chaos imagery from Hebrew cosmology (Psalm 107). Disciples follow but are about to learn what kind of teacher they follow.
Seismos (σεισμός, earthquake-storm) — unusual word for a storm, often used for cosmic upheaval. Jesus sleeps — the unbothered sovereign amid chaos.
'Lord, save us, we are perishing.' Cry of total dependence. The disciples panic; he sleeps. Reversal of typical religious expectation.

'You of little faith' (oligopistoi, ὀλιγόπιστοι) — diagnostic before deliverance. He rebukes (epetimesen, ἐπετίμησεν — same word used for demons) wind and sea. Creation responds to its Lord as demons do.
'What sort of man is this?' The disciples ask the right question. Jonah slept through a storm and was thrown overboard; Jesus sleeps and the storm submits. The contrast is loaded.

Gadarene region — Gentile territory. Two demoniacs from the tombs (Matthew has two where Mark has one — a Matthean stylistic feature). They are so violent the road is unusable. Pure chaos in Gentile space.
'Son of God' — demons recognize him before disciples do. 'Before the time' (pro kairou, πρὸ καιροῦ) — they know their judgment is appointed for an eschatological moment, not yet.
Herd of pigs — unclean animals confirming the Gentile setting. The narrative ironies stack: unclean demons, unclean space, unclean animals.
Demons negotiate — they would rather inhabit pigs than the abyss. Even in defeat they cling to embodied existence.
'Go' — single word, total authority. The herd rushes down the steep bank into the sea and drowns. The chaos of the sea swallows the chaos of the demons — symbolic clearing of Gentile land.
Herdsmen flee to tell. Material loss outweighs spiritual deliverance in their reporting. Witnesses but not converts.
Whole town comes — and asks Jesus to leave. Economic loss matters more to them than two restored men. They prefer demons to disrupted commerce. A sobering reception.
